title: Pelmeni
subtitle: Russian meat dumplings
excerpt: Thin dough rounds filled with seasoned pork and onion, folded and pinched into their characteristic half-moon shape, then boiled and eaten with butter and soured cream or in a bowl of clear broth.
sourceNotes: Pelmeni originate in Siberia and the Urals, where they were made in large batches in winter and frozen outdoors for storage. Documented in Lesley Chamberlain, The Food and Cooking of Eastern Europe (1989) and Elena Molokhovets, A Gift to Young Housewives (1861, modern reprint). They are now a staple across Russia.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze raw on a floured tray, then bag. Cook from frozen in boiling salted water for 8 to 10 minutes.

content.text: Making pelmeni is a task for an afternoon and ideally several pairs of hands. In Russia it is traditionally a family activity — everyone gathered around a table, rolling dough, filling, folding, pinching. The process is slow but meditative, and the reward is a large batch that can be frozen and cooked throughout the winter.

The dough must be thin: 2 mm. Any thicker and the dumpling is heavy and stodgy. The filling should be well-seasoned and slightly loose in texture; dense filling makes the pelmeni tough. Fold the filled circle in half, press the edge firmly to seal, then bring the two corners together and press them — this creates the characteristic bonnet shape.
Mix the flour, salt, egg, and warm water in a bowl until a shaggy dough forms. Turn out and knead for 8 minutes until smooth and elastic. Wrap and rest for 30 minutes.

Mix the pork mince, onion, garlic, salt, and pepper. Keep cold. Roll the dough very thin on a floured surface — about 2 mm. Cut into circles using a 7 to 8 cm cutter or glass. Place a scant teaspoon of filling in the centre of each circle. Fold in half and press the edge firmly to seal, then bring the two corners of the half-moon together and press to form the bonnet shape. Set on a floured tray. Repeat with remaining dough and filling.

Bring a large pot of salted water to a boil. Cook the pelmeni in batches of 12 to 15 for 5 to 6 minutes until they float and the dough is tender. Drain and toss with butter. Serve with soured cream and a grinding of black pepper.

items.cause: Edges not sealed properly, or too much filling
items.fix: Press the edges firmly and ensure no filling touches the border; a little water on the edge before sealing helps it stick
Roll to 2 mm and cook for no more than 6 minutes from fresh; from frozen allow 8 to 10

content.text: Pelmeni are Siberian in origin, related to the Ural-Altaic tradition of filled dumplings that includes Chinese jiaozi, Mongolian buuz, and Georgian khinkali. In Siberia they were historically made at the start of winter and frozen outdoors in their thousands, providing fast food through the cold months. Today they are eaten across Russia and found in every supermarket freezer — but the homemade version, eaten fresh with butter, is another thing entirely.
